National Monsoon Zones reference layer

State-level outline map (Survey of India administrative boundaries, simplified for display), coloured to match the IMD zone graphic you provided ("The Line That Divides India's Summer"). Click a state to filter the district table on the right. Telangana is not separately delineated in this basemap and is shown merged with Andhra Pradesh.

Zone reference image source: India Meteorological Department (IMD), via mausam.imd.gov.in
Next Week Outlook (25 Jun–7 Jul 2026)
IMD Extended Range Forecast covers 25 Jun–1 Jul; the NCEP GFS Ensemble map below extends the picture through 7 Jul.
  • Monsoon advance is expected to push further into the remaining Arabian Sea sector, Maharashtra, Odisha, Jharkhand, Bihar, and parts of Gujarat, Madhya Pradesh & Uttar Pradesh during the week.
  • Rainfall is expected to stay below normal across most of the country; Konkan & Goa, Karnataka, Rayalaseema, Kerala and Tamil Nadu are the exception, trending near-normal to above-normal.
  • Northeast India and the sub-Himalayan belt should see scattered to fairly widespread rain, with isolated heavy-to-very-heavy spells over Arunachal Pradesh, Assam, Meghalaya and Sub-Himalayan West Bengal.
  • A low-probability heatwave signal is flagged for Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Vidarbha (Maharashtra) and north Telangana on some days of the week.
  • Above-normal maximum temperatures are broadly expected across northwest, central, north-peninsular and east India; the rest of the country trends near-normal.
Source: IMD Press Release dated 18.06.2026, "Current Weather Status and Extended Range Forecast for the next two weeks (18 June to 01 July 2026)"
NCEP GFS Ensemble model — 8–14 day precipitation accumulation, 01–07 Jul 2026 (bias-corrected on last 30-day forecast error). Global model product, shown alongside the IMD outlook above to extend the rainfall picture through 7 July.

Sowing guidance — select a condition filter above, or read row-by-row: Normal/Excess/Large Excess districts are favourable for sowing; Deficient districts need monitoring for slow progress; Large Deficient districts face a high chance of delayed sowing. "High-risk" Tur/Urad districts above are those currently Large Deficient or Deficient.
GFS Week-1 Rainfall Forecast
Forecast Period: 26 Jun 2026 – 02 Jul 2026 · NCEP/NOAA GFS model, weekly accumulated rainfall (mm)
Rajasthan
Forecast: ~1.5 mm (area-weighted)
Dominant band: <2 mm (85.8% of area)
Category: Negligible / Dry
Pulse crops: Tur
Sowing impact: Limited — monitor next rainfall spell before committing area
Pulse-growing states outlined in red — hover or tap a state for its forecast read-out. Click the map to open it full-size.
Negligible / Dry (<2mm)Very Light (2–10mm)Light (10–25mm)Moderate (25–50mm)Good (50–100mm)Heavy (100–200mm)Very Heavy (200–300mm)Extremely Heavy (>300mm)
Source: NOAA/NCEP GFS Ensemble, total accumulated precipitation, week of 26 Jun–02 Jul 2026. Model output, not an IMD observational product.

Regional Rainfall Summary

Northwest India

Negligible / Dry
~54.9mm avg · <2mm dominant (72.5%)
  • Dry pockets persist over Rajasthan/Punjab
  • Sowing-limiting in the driest districts

Central India

Light
~60.1mm avg · 10-25mm dominant (22.7%)
  • Monsoon active over MP/Chhattisgarh belt
  • Generally supportive of Kharif sowing

Western Coast

Heavy
~89.7mm avg · 100-150mm dominant (22.3%)
  • Strong monsoon activity along the Konkan coast
  • Watch for localized waterlogging in low-lying fields

East & Northeast

Moderate
~74.3mm avg · 25-50mm dominant (23.8%)
  • Good moisture availability across the east
  • Isolated heavy spells possible in the Northeast

South Peninsula

Light
~28.9mm avg · 10-25mm dominant (41.7%)
  • Moderate, fairly widespread rainfall
  • Generally favourable for crop establishment

Pulse Crop Assessment

StateRainfallPulse CropsImpact
Andhra Pradesh (+Telangana) Light Urad Tur Sowing progressing; additional rainfall beneficial
Chhattisgarh Heavy Urad Excellent moisture availability for crop establishment
Karnataka Light Urad Tur Sowing progressing; additional rainfall beneficial
Madhya Pradesh Light Tur Sowing progressing; additional rainfall beneficial
Maharashtra Light Tur Sowing progressing; additional rainfall beneficial
Rajasthan Negligible / Dry Tur Limited — monitor next rainfall spell before committing area
Uttar Pradesh Very Light — Limited, additional rainfall would help sowing progress

Overall Outlook

GFS Week-1 (26 Jun 2026 – 02 Jul 2026) points to a mixed picture across the pulse-growing belt: Chhattisgarh are tracking towards favourable soil-moisture conditions for Kharif pulse sowing this week. Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh remain on the drier side and would benefit from additional rainfall before sowing area is committed there. Figures above are area-weighted reads off the GFS map itself, not an IMD product — treat them as a directional cross-check alongside the IMD outlook earlier on this page.
